one_line_takeaway: Because MLLMs inherit an LLM text encoder, text-only jailbreaks transfer to them at ~50% ASR essentially regardless of the paired image, so MLLM safety alignment must defend the text channel, not just the image channel.
key_finding: Text-based LLM jailbreak prompts transfer strongly to MLLMs largely independent of the paired image. Across 10 MLLMs, LLM-transfer attacks averaged 50.5% ASR and the whole benchmark averaged 44%; the same prompts hit the MLLMs' LLM text encoders at 68.7% average ASR (64.4% on the 8 original source LLMs). Text-based attacks (50.5%) greatly exceeded the best image-based attack (~30%), and the coefficient of variation across the 4 image types was only 9.0, showing image content barely affects success. Per-model totals ranged 26% to 58.1%; most vulnerable policies were Malware (57.9%) and Economic Harm (53.1%).
core_contribution: The paper introduces two artifacts of its own: (1) RedTeam-2K, a curated dataset of 2,000 harmful queries spanning 16 safety policies drawn from 8 sources (GPT Rewrite, Handcraft, GPT Generate, plus reconstructed data from LLM Jailbreak Study, AdvBench, BeaverTails, Shen et al.'s Question Set, and Anthropic hh-rlhf), and (2) JailBreakV-28K, a benchmark of 28,000 jailbreak text-image test cases built on RedTeam-2K. The central, self-described-as-unexplored research question is whether jailbreak techniques that work on LLMs transfer to multimodal LLMs (MLLMs), since MLLMs embed an LLM as their text encoder. The 28K cases comprise 20,000 text-based 'LLM transfer' attacks (5,000 strong LLM jailbreak prompts each paired with 4 image types) and 8,000 image-based MLLM attacks. Note: the individual attack methods (GCG, Cognitive Overload, PAP, real-world templates, FigStep, Query-Relevant) are prior work the authors reuse to generate the benchmark, not their own inventions — their contribution is the benchmark, the RedTeam-2K query set, and the transferability study.
threat_model: Adversary submits crafted text (and optionally image) inputs to an open-source MLLM to elicit responses to malicious queries that violate safety policies. Text-transfer attacks assume the attacker can apply LLM jailbreak techniques (templates, GCG suffixes, cognitive-overload paraphrasing, persuasion) to the text prompt; image-based attacks (FigStep, Query-Relevant) embed harmful content via typography or query-relevant generated images. The benchmark evaluates black-box robustness of the target MLLM's alignment; the key exposure is that the MLLM's LLM text encoder is not safety-aligned against these transferred prompts. No optimization against the specific target MLLM is required for the text-transfer attacks — prompts selected as strong on source LLMs already generalize.
limitations: Scope is limited to 10 open-source MLLMs (no proprietary/closed models such as GPT-4V or Gemini are evaluated). ASR relies entirely on Llama-Guard as the automated harmfulness judge, so results inherit that judge's error/bias, and no human-agreement or alternative-judge validation is reported. The 'transfer' prompts were pre-selected as strong on 8 source LLMs, so the high MLLM ASRs partly reflect that selection filter rather than arbitrary attacks. The multilingual Cognitive Overload strategy was deliberately excluded. The paper is a benchmark/measurement study — it proposes no defense, only motivates future work. Numbers are dataset/judge-configuration specific and not directly comparable to studies using different judges or safety-category definitions.

datasets_used: RedTeam-2K (2,000 harmful queries, 16 safety policies — introduced by this paper)
JailBreakV-28K (28,000 text-image jailbreak test cases — introduced by this paper: 20,000 text-transfer + 8,000 image-based)
quantitative_results: Average ASR of LLM-transfer attacks across 10 MLLMs = 50.5%
Average ASR of the whole 28K benchmark across 10 MLLMs = 44%
Average ASR of LLM-transfer attacks on the MLLMs' LLM text encoders = 68.7%
Average ASR of transfer prompts on the original 8 source LLMs = 64.4%
Best image-based attack ASR = ~30% (vs 50.5% for text-based)
Coefficient of variation across 4 image types = 9.0 (image type barely affects ASR)
Most vulnerable safety policies: Malware avg ASR 57.9%, Economic Harm avg ASR 53.1%
89,940 candidate jailbreak prompts generated, top 5,000 unique selected
RedTeam-2K avg positive similarity 0.12 (lowest/most diverse in Table 1) vs 0.15-0.33 for prior datasets
Table 4 LLM-encoder ASR example: Vicuna-7B total 92.7% (Template 92.8, Persuade 91.8, Logic 94.6)
baselines_compared: Dataset comparison (Table 1): SafeBench, MM-SafetyBench, Question Set (Chao et al.), LLM Jailbreak Study, AdvBench, MultiJail[English], HarmfulTasks, HarmBench
10 evaluated MLLMs: LLaVA-1.5-7B/13B, InstructBLIP-Vicuna-7B/13B, Qwen-VL-Chat-7B, LLaMA-Adapter-V2-7B, OmniLMM-12B, InfiMM-Zephyr-7B, Bunny-v1.0-3B, InternLM-XComposer2-VL-7B
Attack methods compared: LLM-transfer (Template=GCG+real-world templates, Logic=Cognitive Overload, Persuade=PAP) vs image-based (FigStep, Query-Relevant)
